  • CQC Technical Preparation for SAP Signavio Process Insights and Intelligence (Blog)
  • CQC Deployment Readiness for the Integrated Toolchain (Blog)
  • CQC Deployment Readiness for SAP Signavio Process Manager and Collaboration Hub (Blog)
  • CQC Security Optimization for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Blog)
  • EGI* Support Migration to SAP Integration Suite (Blog)

Discover our workstreams: workstream overviews

Get Involved? 

  • Learn more: visit us on SAP Support Portal. 
  • contact us:  esac@sap.com

Your SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council team

*Continuous Quality Check (CQC): Work with our experts to analyze your system using real data and receive a report with key findings and a clear action plan to improve outcomes and reduce risks.

*Expert-Guided Implementation (EGI): A multi-day, remote, workshop-style session where participants learn to complete a defined activity with the support of experienced SAP service engineers.

entry.title: In Brief - Product Compliance Dangerous Goods Management S/4HANA - End to End Overview
entry.content:
In Brief - Product Compliance Dangerous Goods Management - End to End Overview

Transporting dangerous goods is subject to strict regulatory requirements and increasing scrutiny across global supply chains. Non compliance can lead to serious safety risks, legal consequences, and operational disruptions.

At the same time, many organizations still rely on fragmented processes, manual checks, and inconsistent data, making it difficult to ensure compliance at scale and across functions.

In this video, we illustrate how SAP S/4HANA supports an integrated approach to Dangerous Goods Management as part of Product Compliance. By embedding compliance directly into product master data and connecting it with logistics processes, companies can ensure that relevant regulations are considered consistently from the very beginning.

This enables automated checks during order and delivery processing, improves data transparency, and helps ensure that all required information, documentation, and handling instructions are available where they are needed.

An integrated approach not only reduces compliance risks but also increases efficiency and supports reliable and safe transport execution across markets.

Watch the demo video to see how this can be realized in practice.

In Brief - Product Compliance Dangerous Goods Management - End to End Overview

entry.title: SAP Cloud Integration (CI) for SAP IBP HPA integration: A real-world implementation case study
entry.content:
In one of the previous blog post, SAP Cloud Integration was introduced as new integration option for SAP IBP. Subsequent blog posts covered technical topics for creating integration flows based on SAP delivered packages and flows for different integration scenarios. These blog posts in addition to highlighting why organizations should consider SAP CI as part of their IBP integration strategy, covered fundamental set-up and configuration steps for using it for integrating SAP IBP with S/4 Hana Cloud.

In this blog, we explore a real-world example of an organization using Cloud Integration to overcome complex integration challenges for their IBP HPA implementation. We share details of challenges, key architectural decisions, and lessons learned from this complex supply chain planning implementation. This blog highlights a proven integration approach that offers valuable learnings for organizations navigating similar challenges.

Integration challenges: Distributed landscape

The organization in focus like other large manufacturing organizations operates an extensive global supply chain. Their landscape includes:

  • Multiple ERP system distributed globally
  • Large product portfolio with complex supply chain spread across locations using different systems

The key challenge was to integrate data from multiple SAP and Non-SAP systems. The integration approach needs to be flexible enough to support multiple source system integration with SAP IBP.

Key decision: Cloud Integration or Real Time Integration

One of the most critical decisions in any IBP implementation is to choose the right integration tool for IBP.  From release 2508 onwards, RTI and SAP CI are the two integration tools available to integrate SAP IBP HPA with SAP S4 Hana private cloud.

 

The sample customer’s implementation team made an early decision to go with a standalone CI integration approach for IBP. Some of the drivers for this decision were:

  • Cloud Integration works well with all SAP and non-SAP ERP systems, compared to RTI which is restricted only to S/4HANA Private edition
  • Readily available Cloud Integration expertise within the organization
  • Operational planning is carried out in ERP system, ruling out need for Order based planning in IBP
  • Business followed monthly planning cycle, with delta data load planned daily

Additionally, the availability of prepackaged reusable assets for SAP Cloud Integration supports accelerated implementation and reduces time to value significantly. It provides comprehensive connectivity options for diverse landscape, and it being under active development from SAP keeps your integration architecture future ready. Its web-based centralized monitoring and error handling capabilities help in reducing the overall maintenance cost. Because of these inherent advantages of SAP CI, the implementation team decided to go with standalone SAP CI integration architecture.

As real-time data sync capability was not needed, RTI didn’t offer any additional business value in comparison to CI in this scenario. However, depending on your business needs, Operational planning (OBP) in IBP and real time sync capabilities could be essential, in that case CI can be used in conjunction with RTI. As per SAP guidelines, integration architecture can utilize both the integration technologies following way:

  • RTI used for integrating master data and order data from S/4 Hana Private edition
  • SAP Cloud Integration (CI) used for all other integration scenarios such as Key Figures or other non-standard objects.

Following SAP help portal page helps you find right integration tool for your business needs: Finding the Right Integration Tool | SAP Help Portal

Integration design philosophy: keep interfaces “Lean” and “Scalable”

An important contributing factor to the successful implementation of CI for SAP IBP was to keep interfaces simple. The customer's implementation team followed a clear guiding principle that Cloud Integration (CI) should be used only for transporting data from one system to another. It should not be used for deriving any business logic. Cloud Integration (CI) is best used for:

  • Data transportation
  • Data format transformation and technical validation
  • Security handling

Business rules, process logic, and calculations were handled in the source or target system. This approach of preprocessing data in source or target system, made sure CI flows are simple, maintainable and reusable.

Preprocessing of data in source system:

To keep the core and IBP Add-on clean on the S/4 Hana, the implementation team decided to use extended CDS views for data preprocessing in S/4 Hana. Cloud integration flows read data directly from CDS views, avoiding modification of extractors and therefore sticking to clean core best practices.

How it works: Multi layered CDS views were created for preprocessing data in S/4 Hana:

  1. Reuse layer CDS views: Built on IBP add-on staging tables and raw tables (for additional data), this layer provided filtered data. As these CDS view also included data from raw tables, any custom data fields not available in IBP add-on staging tables were captured in these CDS views
  2. Consumption layer CDS views: This is primarily used for renaming S/4 fields to IBP fields. Reuse layer CDS views served as input for these CDS views. CI flows read data directly from these CDS views.

Above approach along with Change Data Capture (CDC) capability for CDS extraction allows delta loads for master data and S/4 Hana execution transaction data.

Reusable integration flows in CI:

SAP CI provides prepackaged reusable integration flows, that can be used to integrate all master data and transaction data types with minimal configuration changes. For the sample customer, data integration is executed by running a single prepackaged reusable CI iflow. One reusable integration flow handles all integration scenarios – all master data and key figures, with the help of flexible parameters. These parameters define:

  • Source CDS view to read in S/4 Hana
  • Target Master data and Key figures in IBP
  • Transfer mode: delta or full load

By sticking to preprocessing within the source system using CDS views and integrating using reusable flow, solution is flexible to accommodate future requirements. Any future business requirements can be handled by extending CDS views in the source system, avoiding any changes in the integration layer.

Integration with non-SAP system:

To integrate non-SAP ERP systems, data from multiple ERP systems is first integrated into a Data warehousing system. In Data warehousing system similar CDS view dependent process is used to preprocess data and then a reusable CI flow is used to pull data into IBP. This unified approach means consistent data structure regardless of the source system type.

Back integration to S4 Hana system:

IBP add-on staging tables enable a fire and forget approach for integrating planning output data from IBP to S/4 Hana. CI delivers data to S/4 IBP add-on tables and S/4 process them independently.  

With this lean integration approach, figure below shows high level data flow between different systems:

 

 Key takeaways: Some of the key learnings from this successful implementation of CI integration are:

  • Evaluating business requirements to select the right integration tool for your implementation. Depending on specific scenarios, CI only integration strategy can be effective.
  • Keep integration lean and focus on data transport only. Push transformation and business logic in source and target systems
  • Availability of prepackaged integration flow helps in accelerated implementation, and results in scalable and low maintenance integration solution

entry.title: What is SAP plant reallocation?
entry.content:
If you’ve worked in SAP transformation for long enough, you quickly realise that organisational structures rarely stay static for very long. Companies acquire new businesses. They divest underperforming ones. Manufacturing footprints change. Supply chains get redesigned. Tax structures evolve. Geopolitical events suddenly make one region less attractive than another.

Over the past few years, those changes have arguably accelerated even further as organisations respond to inflation, supply chain disruption, rising labour costs and geopolitical uncertainty. In many cases, businesses are being forced to rethink where they manufacture products, where they hold inventory and how their operational footprint is structured.

This often creates a very specific SAP challenge. A business may decide to move manufacturing from one country to another, consolidate multiple production facilities into one location or transfer responsibility for a plant from one legal entity to another. From a business perspective, this may seem like a relatively straightforward organisational change. From an SAP perspective, however, it can be significantly more complicated.

Enter the SAP plant reallocation project.

What is SAP plant reallocation?

At a high level, SAP plant reallocation refers to moving an existing plant from one company code to another, following some kind of organisational restructuring. While this sounds relatively simple on paper, the challenge is that plants sit at the centre of many critical SAP processes and are heavily embedded within both logistics and finance functions.

A plant is typically linked to material master records, inventory, production orders, purchase orders, sales orders, valuation records, warehouse processes and financial postings. Over time, that plant builds up a significant amount of transactional history and operational dependencies. This means organisations cannot simply change a configuration setting and move on. They need a way to move the plant without breaking operational processes, losing historical data or creating major disruption for the business.

Why do organisations need plant reallocation?

The reasons for undertaking plant reallocations vary. In some cases, organisations are moving manufacturing operations to lower-cost regions to improve profitability. In others, businesses are redesigning supply chains to reduce dependency on specific geographies after experiencing disruptions in recent years.

Mergers and acquisitions can also create scenarios where newly acquired manufacturing locations need to be aligned to existing legal structures. Similarly, divestitures may require certain plants to move into new legal entities while others remain with the original business. Tax, regulatory and customs changes can also force businesses to rethink how their operational footprint is structured.

Regardless of the reason, the SAP challenge tends to remain the same: how do you move a plant from one company code to another without causing unnecessary operational disruption?

Why is this difficult in SAP?

This is where things become technically challenging. Once a plant has been active for a long period of time, it becomes connected to a wide range of master and transactional data. This can include stock, open purchase orders, open production orders, batch records, warehouse management data, valuation records, financial postings and reporting structures. Organisations also need to think carefully about historical reporting, audit requirements and financial continuity. Even if a plant moves to a new legal entity, the business still needs access to historical transactions and reporting data.

The traditional approach

Typically, many organisations have handled this challenge using a manual approach. This often involves freezing activity in the original plant, creating a new plant under the new company code and then manually recreating the relevant master data and transactional objects. Inventory may need to be transferred manually, open transactions may need to be recreated and finance teams often need to perform significant reconciliation work to ensure reporting remains accurate.

While this approach can work, it is time-consuming, it introduces operational downtime and creates significant manual effort. It can also introduce unnecessary risk, particularly when large volumes of transactions need to be recreated manually. And from the perspective of the business user, it creates a break between the transactional activity in the new company and the historical activity in the previous company code.

The table-based transformation approach

A more modern approach involves transforming the underlying SAP data model at table level rather than manually rebuilding everything from scratch. Instead of creating a completely new plant and migrating operational activity manually, organisations can use landscape transformation tools to update the relevant SAP tables so that the existing plant reflects the new organisational structure.

This approach typically involves updating plant assignments, company code relationships, valuation records and other dependent logistics and finance objects. Because these objects are often deeply interconnected, this requires a structured and controlled transformation process. This is where specialist tools such as Natuvion Data Conversion Suite (DCS) are often used.

Landscape transformation technology enables organisations to analyse the impacted data objects, identify dependencies between tables and execute the required transformation in a controlled way, at scale and at pace. Rather than manually rebuilding operational processes, the software helps organisations transform existing structures while preserving transactional history and reducing manual effort. You might think of this as business redesign from the inside out.

This can significantly reduce downtime, minimise disruption to the business and allow organisations to adapt far more quickly when operational changes are required.

Final thoughts

Plant reallocation in SAP is not something organisations should underestimate. Even with specialist transformation tooling, careful planning is required to understand which data objects are impacted, how integrations may be affected and what historical information needs to remain accessible after the move. Testing is also critical to ensure operational processes continue functioning as expected once the transformation has been completed.

Plant reallocation may not be discussed as frequently as carve-outs, cloud moves or S/4HANA migrations, but I suspect it will become increasingly common as businesses continue adapting their operational footprint in response to changing market conditions. As organisations become more agile, their SAP landscapes need to become more flexible as well. The traditional manual approach can still work, but table-based transformation approaches are increasingly offering organisations a quicker and less disruptive way to respond to change.

If you would like to read more on this topic, you can do so here.

entry.title: Cloud ERP SCM Product-centric Customer Council - Shaping the Future of AI-Powered Supply Chain
entry.content:
by Julia Zinovieva (Customer Officer Cloud ERP SCM)

Cloud ERP SCM Product-centric Customer Council - Shaping the Future of AI-Powered Supply Chain

On April 14–16, the Cloud ERP SCM team presented the latest developments and AI innovations at the Product-centric Customer Council in Berlin. Over 2.5 days, customers and SAP experts came together for an exciting and inspiring exchange of insights across key solution areas including SAP Integrated Product Development (IPD), Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC), Available-to-Promise (ATP), Asset Management, and PP/DS.

The agenda combined strategic perspectives, product deep dives, and interactive formats. Product Management did an excellent job explaining the roadmaps in their respective areas, demonstrating AI use cases, and enabling deep, fruitful discussions during breakout sessions. These sessions created a valuable opportunity to hear firsthand from customers – their perspectives on the presented functionality, as well as their concerns, expectations, and ideas around AI use cases and future innovations.

The council was opened with an executive overview of the Cloud ERP SCM strategy from Vatsan Govindarajan (CPO Cloud ERP SCM), setting the strategic direction and context for the innovations and discussions that followed.

AI was a central theme throughout the event. Sessions such as:

  • Insights into Combined Orders and AI-supported Backorder Processing led by Andreas Krause and Sarah Deser
  • Driving Efficiency in Inventory Management Through AI led by Darius Nowak, Stefanie Bierbach and Bernd Rödel
  • AI Support for Supplier Certificates and Complaint Processing  led by Stefan Resag

illustrated how SAP is moving beyond traditional automation toward intelligent, outcome-driven supply chain execution.
The sessions demonstrated how agents can replace manual, time-intensive, and reactive work across multiple applications through proactive assessments and intelligent recommendations – freeing up capacity to focus on higher-value, critical tasks.

Customer contributions, including “Acceleron – AI Use Case Test Automation”,  further reinforced the importance of co-innovation and real-world validation.

At the same time, core business capabilities remained in focus where colleagues presented updates in AVC such as Solution Bundling app, innovations for Asset Management​ and the direction for Advanced Production Planning ​in cloud.

Breakout sessions enabled open dialogue and deeper collaboration, ensuring that customer feedback directly shapes SAP’s product direction. In my interactive AI Talk, we gathered valuable insights into customer entry barriers for AI adoption such as cost and licensing model, agent accountability, received immediate feedback on the demonstrated AI use cases, and gained a deeper understanding of customers’ vision and expectations for AI capabilities across the supply chain.

Thanks to all participants for their openness, engagement, and valuable contributions. 

And a big thank you to the core organizational team Markus Oertelt and Fernanda Rodrigues for the smooth organization, a well-curated agenda, a picturesque venue, and outstanding ongoing support that made this event a truly seamless and inspiring experience!

entry.title: What is variant configuration and when should it be used?
entry.content:
What is Variant Configuration (and When Should You Use It)?

If you’ve ever tried to order coffee with more than three modifiers—extra hot, half-caf, oat milk, two pumps hazelnut—you already understand product complexity. Now imagine doing that with forklifts, machinery, or electronics and trying to keep your master data from bursting into tears. That’s where SAP Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC) comes in: it’s the difference between a chaotic spreadsheet of doom and a tidy, rules-driven model that says, “Don’t worry, I’ve got this.”

 

The Big Idea (No Bullet Points, Promise)

Advanced Variant Configuration lets you represent countless product possibilities using one configurable material. Instead of creating a separate material for every “diesel + 45 performance + 3‑ton + blue + camera” combo, you define characteristics (like engine type, performance, tonnage, color, and accessories), group them logically, and wire them together with rules that keep things sensible. You also build a Super BOM that contains all potential components and a Super Routing with all potential operations. The system then does the heavy lifting: given a customer’s selection, it picks the right parts and steps automatically. You keep your master data lean, your sales team fast, and your production team off your back.

How It Works (In Real Life)

It starts with modeling. You create a configurable material, define the characteristics your customers care about, and organize them into a class made for configuration. You then build one master bill of materials—the Super BOM—that includes every component any variant might need, and a routing that covers all possible operations. Finally, you add object dependencies—rules that clarify what goes with what, so “electric + 55 performance + 5‑ton” doesn’t sneak past quality like a cat through an open door.

When a sales order comes in, the salesperson selects the values the customer wants. SAP looks at those choices, applies the rules, explodes the Super BOM, and produces a variant-specific structure and routing that production can actually build. No guesswork. No “which one did we mean?” Slack threads. Just the right components, every time.

A Forklift Walks Into a Showroom…

Picture a forklift product line with three drive types (diesel, gas, electric), multiple performance levels, tonnage from one to five tons, several colors, and a menu of accessories. The number of combinations balloons fast—into the tens of thousands if you let it. Without AVC, you’d need a material for each variant, plus a BOM and routing for each. Congratulations, you now manage a small library of nearly identical records that differ on line ten, footnote two. With AVC, you model the product once, capture the logic once, and reuse it endlessly. You can even define commonly sold configurations as material variants for stocking or quick quoting, while still relying on the same brains behind the scenes.

When You Should (Absolutely) Use Advanced Variant Configuration

Use AVC when your products are like LEGO sets—modular, customizable, and prone to enthusiastic customers wanting “just one more piece.” If your sales process involves choosing from a menu of features, if your master data threatens to spread like ivy, or if you need to introduce new options without rebuilding everything from scratch, AVC will save your roadmap (and your weekends). It’s especially handy in configure-to-order and engineer-to-order environments, where enforcing valid combinations isn’t a nice-to-have; it’s the difference between smooth production and emergency meetings.

Why Teams End Up Loving It

First, there’s the master data diet: one configurable material instead of thousands. Maintenance becomes manageable, and the risk of copy‑paste errors drops dramatically. Sales gets faster because the system helps them choose valid, buildable options without phoning a friend in engineering. Production gets exactly what was promised, complete with the right components and operations. And finance appreciates that configuration rules can gently steer choices toward cost‑appropriate combinations. In short, AVC keeps everyone aligned, which, in most companies, is rarer than a quiet inbox.

What You Need in Place

Success with AVC starts with a clear, honest picture of your product. That means well-defined characteristics that reflect how you actually sell and build, not how someone wished you did two reorgs ago. Your Super BOM and Super Routing should be comprehensive and well-structured, so dependencies can do their job cleanly. Each configurable material needs a configuration profile to control behavior, and your team should be comfortable with the Product Modeling Environment (PME/PMEVC) to maintain logic. Finally, make sure your modelers speak the language of dependencies—preconditions, selection conditions, procedures, and constraints—because that’s how your product rules come alive. If you’re moving to Advanced Variant Configuration (AVC), expect better performance and more modeling power with the same core principles—like upgrading from a trusty bike to an e‑bike that still pedals, but flies uphill.

The Payoff (and a Gentle Nudge)

Advanced Variant Configuration turns option overload into a controlled, elegant model. It keeps your master data slim, your configurations valid, and your lead times realistic. If your product line is already complex—or your roadmap suggests it soon will be—AVC is the practical way to offer customers choice without breeding chaos. A great way to start is to pick one product line, define the must-have characteristics, draft a Super BOM that mirrors reality, and pilot it with a few real orders. Iterate, tighten the rules, and watch the noise go down while speed and confidence go up.

 

If you’ve made it this far, congrats—you now speak fluent “configurable product.” The next best step is small and practical: pick one product line, sketch the must‑have characteristics, and let a pilot model prove the value. From there, you’ll refine the rules, trim the noise, and watch quoting and production get measurably smoother. May your dependencies be tidy, your BOMs be super, and your lead times politely short.

entry.title: Your Innovation Boost: A Shot of SAP at Hannover Messe
entry.content:
What have ginger shots and SAP in common? 

You have no idea? Then let’s find out! 

Every year in April, all eyes turn to Hannover Messe, a place buzzing with new ideas, groundbreaking innovations, and of course, AI!  

This year marked my very first time attending and honestly, nothing could have prepared me for what I walked into. The sheer scale of it left me speechless. We’re talking about massive booths that don’t just display products but tell entire stories through their complexity and stunning visual design. The look and feel of everything around me was nothing short of spectacular, a perfect blend of cutting-edge technology and creative presentation.
Imagine crowded halls, enormous booths showcasing the latest in robotics and technology, all brought together in one breathtaking area.

And right in the middle of it all, you’ll find SAP!

The Vibe of the World’s Industrial Heartbeat  

Hannover Messe is more than just a trade fair; it’s the epicentre of industrial transformation. The energy is electric. Everywhere you look, you see passion and progress. This year’s theme, "Think Tech Forward," perfectly captures that feeling. It’s all about looking ahead and embracing the technologies that will shape the future of industry. 
 
The main buzz was around how digitalization, AI, and sustainability can drive real change. The focus was on practical solutions that help companies stay competitive while also protecting our planet. Artificial Intelligence was a huge topic, especially how it’s being woven directly into production with autonomous robots and Generative AI. 
And walking through the halls, you could actually feel that vision coming to life. 

 

Our Role in the Digital Factory 

And SAP? We’re the digital thread that weaves through it all. While amazing machines and robots were on display everywhere, we provide the intelligent software that connects the entire value chain, from design to operations, to build the resilient and sustainable businesses of the future. 

The SAP booth this year was less "corporate exhibition stand" and more "place you accidentally spend two hours at". There was a lot to see, a lot to try, and something worth sticking around for at the very end. 

 

Highlights from the SAP Booth 

AI That Actually Makes Sense 

Let’s be honest, AI is everywhere right now. But at the SAP booth, it wasn’t just a buzzword on a slide. The demos showed how solutions like SAP IBP and SAP S/4HANA are using embedded AI to genuinely make supply chains smarter through better forecasting, sharper inventory planning, and faster reactions to disruptions. 

Less "AI for the sake of AI." More "AI that actually helps you sleep at night." 

Don’t just take our word for it, it was fantastic to see our customer, SMA Solar Technology, on stage explaining how they automate their planning processes with SAP Integrated Business Planning.  You want to know more about it? Just follow this link.

Knowing What’s Going On, in real time 

Supply chain visibility was another major theme, and for good reason. Nobody wants surprises anymore, at least not the kind that involve empty shelves or delayed shipments. SAP demonstrated how its solutions give companies a real-time view across their entire network, enabling them to spot problems early and react before they escalate.  

It’s the kind of capability that sounds simple but changes everything. 

 

So, What About That Ginger Shot? 

Now, back to our initial question. The final product coming off our high-tech, digitally connected production line was a freshly bottled ginger shot, ready to take with you! 
It might sound like a small gimmick, but it was the perfect metaphor. 

Because the whole SAP booth experience was built exactly like a well-run supply chain. Every step connected, every touchpoint intentional, everything building toward something. You moved through planning, visibility, sustainability, AI. Each piece linking to the next. And then, right at the end of the chain, a physical reward was waiting. 

So, a visit to our booth didn’t just give you a metaphorical boost of innovation; you could walk away with a real one. 

More information about the Story of a Ginger Shot and of course about SAP IBP.

 

See You Next Year! 

Hannover Messe 2026 was a lot.  
A lot of inspiration, a lot of conversations, a lot of "wait, can you show me that again?" moments. And a surprisingly good ginger shot. If you were there, you know the feeling. If not, there’s always next year.

entry.title: Help Keep SAP IBP #1: Your Product Experience Matters
entry.content:
Product Experience Survey - Help Us Make SAP IBP Work Better for You

 When the Product Experience Survey appears in your SAP IBP application, it’s your chance to share what you like, what’s working well and what isn’t. 

We’re looking to understand where you struggle in your daily work—those tasks that take longer than they should or cause friction. We also want to know which features truly make your life easier and work well for you, plus how we can improve your overall experience with SAP IBP applications. Here is a link to a video showing you how to launch the PX Survey in SAP IBP. 

How the PX Survey Works

The PX survey appears directly in your app or can be started manually when you have feedback to share. Your responses are linked to the specific application you’re using, so our development teams get targeted, actionable insights.

 

Where to Find It:

For the Fiori Launchpad you’ll find the survey in the upper right corner using the speech bubble with a smiley icon.

Alternatively you can also use the SAP IBP add-in for Microsoft Excel:

In the IBP Menu goto About -> give Feedback

What’s Included

The survey includes a Product Satisfaction score, Usefulness and Ease of Use ratings, and a comment field for additional details.

 

 

Getting the Most from Your Feedback

Do:

  • Respond when the survey appears—it only takes a moment
  • Focus your comments on the specific app mentioned

Don’t:

  • Include personal information in comments
  • Leave generic responses—specific feedback is more helpful

 

Your Input Makes a Difference

Your feedback helps us understand what’s working and what needs attention. When you take a moment to respond, you’re helping improve the daily experience for yourself and other users.

The next time you see the PX survey, consider sharing your thoughts—it’s one of the most direct ways to influence how SAP IBP evolves.

Reference:

  • Video showing you how to launch the PX Survey in SAP IBP

entry.title: Lessons from the field: What Early Adopters of SAP IBP’s Harmonized Planning Area learned
entry.content:
In September 2025, SAP launched an Early Adopter Care (EAC) program for customers implementing the Harmonized Planning Area (HPA) in SAP IBP. Over 7+ months, 15+ companies across industries joined weekly knowledge-exchange sessions with SAP Product Management, Development, and expert consultants — covering integration architecture, data model deep-dives, and go-live preparation. This blog distills those lessons for the benefit of a wider IBP user community. 

 The Participants — A diverse cross-industry group 

The program attracted 15+ companies from Automotive, Food & Beverage, Life Sciences, Chemicals, High Tech, Consumer Goods, and Agriculture — spanning EMEA and Americas, from mid-size players to global multinationals. Planning scope ranged from a single module to five deployed simultaneously. Integration strategies spanned the full spectrum: RTI-only, CI-only and hybrid. 

Why the Participants joined the initiative: 

Participants joined the HPA EAC for different reasons, but several themes emerged repeatedly: 

  • Greenfield HPA: Starting fresh on IBP and choosing HPA from day one rather than implementing a legacy planning area. 
  • New business unit rollout: Companies already on SAPIBP1 piloting HPA in a second business unit before broader migration. 
  • APO replacement: Early access to understand gaps and plan the transition away from SAP APO. 
  • Exclusive capabilities: Features like harmonized scenarios, vendor-managed inventory exist only in HPA — no alternative for companies with these requirements. 
  • Product influence: A direct channel to SAP Product Management to raise requirements and shape the roadmap while HPA was still maturing.  

Integration decisions — RTI, CI, or both? 

One of the most impactful decisions every HPA customer faces is their integration strategy. The EAC revealed that there is no one-size-fits-all answer — and that most customers end up with a hybrid approach, at least initially. 

The Three Options: 

Technology 

Best for 

Limitations 

RTI (Real-Time Integration) 

Standard master data, OBP transactions, vendor data, delta integration 

No public cloud support; limited to SAP systems; plant-independent requirements not supported 

CI (Cloud Integration) 

Multi-ERP landscapes, non-SAP systems, flexible transformations, public cloud 

Application job template integration was not available during the EAC program 

CIDS (Cloud Integration Data Services) 

Existing implementations, custom enrichment, backward compatibility 

Legacy technology; no longer bundled for new customers; limited tracing 

 

Case Study 1: The CI-Only Approach: A customer made the bold decision to use Cloud Integration exclusively — no RTI at all. Their rationale: 

  • RTI does not work with public cloud deployments or non-SAP ERP systems 
  • They integrate with 4+ different ERP systems globally — RTI only connects to SAP 
  • Internal expertise for CI was more available than for RTI 
  • CI enables trial operations before full S/4HANA migration 

Their architecture: Custom CDS views in S/4HANA handles all data transformation. CI flows are kept “dumb” — pure data transportation with no business logic. This separation of concerns makes debugging and maintenance straightforward. 

Case Study 2: The Hybrid Approach: This customer uses RTI for base master data integration (the standard objects that RTI handles well) and enriches with CIDS for additional attributes that require custom logic on the S/4HANA side. This is a pragmatic approach when you need RTI’s delta capabilities but also have custom fields that RTI bodies don’t cover. 

Key learning — CI vs. CIDS: One customer who migrated from CIDS to SAP CI was really happy with migration. They cited better flexibility, superior tracing, no data agent performance issues, and easier team collaboration since CI skills are not limited to IBP specialists. 

RTI recommendation from SAP: SAP’s integration strategy recommendation for new customers is to go RTI first. This gives customers delta integration capabilities which is not available with time-series methods. RTI also future-proofs the integration — if customer starts with time series and later adopts OBP, the RTI integration is already available. 

Value realization: 

Despite being early adopters on a maturing platform, participants reported concrete value across several dimensions: 

First Customer Live on HPA: In March 2026, a participant became one of the first customers to go live on the Harmonized Planning Area — with SOP, Inventory Optimization, and Time Series Optimizer all active simultaneously. Some of the key values realized by these early adopters are: 

  • Configuration simplification: Customers discovered that with introduction of IBP_CONVERSION function, HPA eliminates the need of helper key figures and additional planning levels for UOM conversion. The system converts automatically without any helpers KFs. Pre-configured conversions cover most use cases. 
  • Unified planning model: Participants who activated both time-series and order-based planning in a single HPA confirmed the core value proposition: consistent results across both planning methods, unified key figures, and no reconciliation required between separate planning areas. One participant preparing for a June 2026 go-live is running TS + OBP + Inventory Optimization + S&OP + Demand & Response Planning — all in one planning area. 

Challenges and concerns raised 

The EAC program is valuable precisely because participants surface real challenges — not just success stories. Here are the most significant concerns raised, presented honestly for the benefit of future adopters. 

Some of the key concerns raised by participants: 

  • Subnetwork filtering in TS optimizer: One consumer goods company cannot limit the time-series optimizer to a specific factory — it runs across the entire global supply chain. Without subnetwork filtering (planned for 2608), planners cannot run optimization at their scope.  
  • Cross-location vendor mapping: An automotive company operating in 60–70 countries discovered that RTI’s inbound mapping allows only one vendor code per source location. With multiple subsidiaries ordering from the same distribution center using different vendor codes, they cannot build their supply network.  
  • Scheduling agreements as source of supply: Critical for automotive planning but not available in HPA until 2608. 

 How SAP supported EAC participants:  

One of the defining characteristics of the EAC format is that customer challenges are shared directly with the product managers and developers who can act on them. SAP team responded to customer concerns by updating product roadmap. Some features committed to Roadmap based on customer inputs are: 

Customer needs

SAP response 

Planned release 

Subnetwork filtering in TS optimizer 

Confirmed on roadmap 

IBP 2608

Scheduling agreements for Response & Supply 

Confirmed on roadmap 

IBP 2608 

Cross-location vendor mapping in RTI 

Roadmap to extend mapping table with role and location info 

IBP 2608 or later

Look-up logic for calendars/attributes 

Delivered 

IBP 2602 

Receiving calendar hierarchy inheritance 

Part of hierarchical lookup feature 

IBP 2602 

 Migration Support announced:  SAP7F Migration EAC: A dedicated EAC program for brownfield customers migrating from SAP7F to HPA, is planned to start with the 2605 release.  If you are interested, please register with following link : Influence Opportunity Homepage - Customer Influence

Best Practice content delivered: In direct response to EAC participant requests, SAP delivered executable best practice content in IBP 2511 — including planning views, analytic stories, and planning profiles that customers could use immediately rather than building from scratch. 

Real-Time issue resolution: Pattern observed throughout the EAC: When customers raised specific technical issues (e.g., attribute naming bugs, OKF behavior questions, lot-for-lot procedure gaps), SAP development teams often investigated and responded within 1–2 sessions — far faster than the standard support ticket process. 

Looking forward — Recommendations for future adopters 

Drawing on seven months of EAC discussions, here are the distilled recommendations for customers considering HPA adoption: 

  1. Start small with partial-scope copy : You do not need to adopt every HPA module at once. When copying I_SAPIBP2, select only what you need — Demand Planning and IO is a common starting point. Expand your footprint as you gain confidence. 
  2. Choose your integration strategy early: The RTI vs. CI decision has cascading effects on your architecture, team skills, and timeline.  
  3. Leverage coexistence for phased transition: HPA and SAP7F planning areas can run side-by-side on the same tenant using separate logical systems. This means you can validate HPA in a contained scope before decommissioning legacy areas — reducing risk significantly. 
  4. Plan for the upgrade lifecycle from day one: When you copy I_SAPIBP2, keep the original sample planning area untouched as a reference for future comparison. When new releases deliver additional content, use the planning area comparison tool to identify deltas and merge selectively. 
  5. Engage with SAP early: Customers in the EAC consistently received faster issue resolution, influenced the product roadmap directly, and benefited from peer learning that would have taken months to discover independently. If an EAC program exists for a capability you’re adopting, join it. 
  6. Best practice content Is your accelerator: Since IBP 2511, SAP has delivered executable planning views, analytic stories, and job profiles for HPA. Use them as your starting point rather than building from scratch. EAC participants who used the pre-release content reported significant time savings in their setup. 

Summary: 

 HPA is where SAP IBP is heading, and the early adopters in this program showed the way. They validated that the model works, the integration options are viable, and the value proposition is real — leaving a clear path for those who follow.

entry.title: Top 12 Sapphire Sessions for Supply Chain Planners and Decision-Makers You Should not Miss!
entry.content:
At SAP SAPPHIRE 2026 in Orlando, there will be a packed agenda for all supply chain planners and decision-makers. Create your conference agenda now, to ensure you do not miss an important session! Hear business leaders from Corning, E.J. Gallo, Georgia-Pacific, Metrie, Microsoft, Mohawk, Owens & Minor, Takeda and SAP talk about their journey towards decision-centric planning with SAP Integrated Business Planning and AI!

 

MONDAY

Building a resilient supply chain with characteristics-based planning | SCM1735

Overview Session

Eric Simonson, Director - Product Marketing SAP IBP, SAP AMERICA, INC.

Discover how characteristics-based planning with the SAP S/4HANA Supply Chain solution for supply and demand segmentation helps align planning with product and market attributes to effectively meet demands. Explore how to boost service, align business priorities, and stay resilient amid supply chain disruptions in real use cases across industries.

  • Monday, May 11, 2:00 PM - 2:20 PM EDT

 

Turning planning into a proactive growth engine with SAP IBP and AI | IND1380

Customer Success Story

Stefan Weisenberger, Global VP, IBU Director Mill Products & Mining, SAP SE

Mark Witiuk, Director, Integrated Business Planning, Metrie

Patrick Weil, IT Director, Metrie Canada Ltd

Metrie transformed supply planning for 15,000 SKUs with forecasting and inventory optimization in the SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP) solution. It cut inventory by 7.8% and lost orders from stockouts by 33%, improving “on-time in-full” from 92.5% to over 95%. Hear about its Joule solution proof of concept and plans to use AI in SAP IBP.

    • Monday, May 11, 4:00 PM - 4:20 PM EDT

 

TUESDAY

Corning’s supply chain planning transformation with SAP IBP and SAP S/4HANA | SCM1418

Customer Success Story

Aravind Balasubramaniam, IT Sr. Manager, SC Portfolio, Corning Incorporated

Shashidhar Pabba, IT Manager Supply Ch, Corning Incorporated

Jim Lichtenwalter, Senior Producer, ASUG

Corning modernized supply chain planning using the SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP) solution with SAP S/4HANA and legacy ERP systems. This enhanced demand, supply, and inventory planning, enabled integration across platforms through a phased approach, delivered measurable value, and prepared the company for a planning transformation.

This session is hosted by ASUG.

    • Tuesday, May 1211:30 AM - 12:15 PM EDT

 

Strategy for adopting AI in SAP IBP and preparing for the rise of agents | SCM2130

Strategy Talk

Stephan Kreipl, Head of Product Management SAP IBP, SAP SE

Everyone is talking about AI in supply chain planning, but what are practical steps for adopting AI? Learn about the AI use cases that are currently available in the SAP Integrated Business Planning solution and strategies to adopt them. Get a peek into the coming agentic AI use cases and learn how to get ready for them.

Watch on demand post-event

    • Tuesday, May 12, 11:30 AM - 11:50 AM EDT

 

How supply chain leaders are pursuing a decision-centric planning approach | SCM1738

Customer Panel Discussion

Lori Harner, Global Vice President, Product Marketing, SAP AMERICA, INC.

Nitin Murali, Vice President, Supply Chain Excellence, E. & J. Gallo Winery

Dhaval Desai, Partner Engineering Manager, Microsoft Corporation

Robert Lecinski, Director, Digital SC & Capabilities Dev., Owens & Minor

Chris White, Director Supply Chain Planning, Mohawk

Hear from industry leaders who are using SAP’s AI-enabled decision intelligence solution and AI agents to orchestrate workflows, surface insights, and make data-driven decisions based on AI-supported scenario simulations. Experience rich process integration in a unified UI for modern planners and decision-makers.

    • Tuesday, May 12, 1:00 PM - 1:45 PM EDT

 

Dynamically adjusting supply chains: Orchestrate, adapt, and outperform | KEY2569

Keynote

Dominik Metzger, President Supply Chain Management, SAP SE

Rebecca Kaufmann, SVP, Head of Enterprise Process Platforms, Takeda Pharmaceuticals International AG

CEOs see supply chains as strategic assets—and COOs and chief supply chain officers must deliver. SAP software unites AI, contextual data, and apps to reimagine operations. From a user experience enhanced by the Joule solution to agent orchestration, transform your supply chain into a resilient, high-performance engine.

Watch on demand post-event

  • Tuesday, May 12, 2:00 PM - 2:45 PM EDT

 

Getting a holistic view of data with SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP IBP | SCM1963

Customer Success Story

Greg Capps, SAP Security & Controls Lead, Georgia-Pacific LLC

Emily Gettelfinger, Director Integrated Business Planning, Georgia-Pacific LLC

SAP S/4HANA often contains rich data for multiple planning scenarios, yet the user experience can be fragmented, demanding cross-functional ERP knowledge. To overcome this, manufacturer Georgia-Pacific LLC implemented the SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP) solutions to address complex planning and forecasting needs.

This session is hosted by ASUG.

    • Tuesday, May 12, 3:00 PM - 3:45 PM EDT

 

WEDNESDAY

Explore what is possible with supply chain planning reimagined | SCM1737

Strategy Talk

Lori Harner, Global Vice President, Product Marketing, SAP AMERICA, INC.

Laura Tozzo, AI Product Lead SAP IBP, SAP SE

See how SAP’s AI-supported decision intelligence platform and AI agents orchestrate workflows, surface insights, and autonomously resolve exceptions across all planning horizons. Gain insights into new embedded and extensible AI capabilities, decision-centric scenario simulations, and how AI agents help leaders make fast, data-driven decisions.

    • Wednesday, May 13, 11:00 AM - 11:20 AM EDT

 

Road map for SAP Integrated Business Planning | SCM1736

Road Map Review

Stephan Kreipl, Head of Product Management SAP IBP, SAP SE

Understand the 12–18 month road map for SAP Integrated Business Planning that translates strategy into execution, highlighting planning agents, a unified planner workspace, and a new harmonized planning area. Get a clear view of our planning investment priorities, shaped by customer and partner feedback, and what they mean for your organization.

  • Wednesday, May 13, 11:30 AM - 12:15 PM EDT

 

AI-enabled supply chain planning at Illumina | SCM1801

Customer Success Story

Aparna Seksaria, Global Director, Life Sciences IBU, SAP

Leon Trevett, Enterprise Transformation Leader, Illumina

Supply chains face service pressure, capital constraints, and supplier complexity. Illumina Inc. built an AI-enabled, multi-echelon planning foundation using the SAP Integrated Business Planning and SAP Business Network Supply Chain Collaboration solutions, reducing excess and obsolete inventory, meeting SLAs, and improving supplier collaboration.

This session is hosted by ASUG.

  • Wednesday, May 13, 11:30 AM - 12:15 PM EDT

 

Anticipating and responding to demand with supply chain transformation | SCM2139

Customer Success Story

Leah Knight, SAP Business Network for Supply Chain Product Marketing, SAP

Hear how a consumer product company uses the SAP Integrated Business Planning solution and SAP Business Network to optimize inventory and anticipate and respond to changing demand. Learn how collaboration with contract manufacturers, co-packers, and upstream suppliers helps reduce excess inventory and enhance customer service.

    • Wednesday, May 13, 2:00 PM - 2:20 PM EDT

 

Transformation to the new harmonized planning area | SCM1872

Strategy Talk

Marco Palumbo, SAP Deutschland SE &Co.KG

Pramod Mane, Sr. Director, Product Management SAP IBP, SAP Labs, LLC. (Palo Alto)

Harmonized planning enabled by the SAP Integrated Business Planning solution replaces siloed systems where demand, supply, inventory, and financial planning operated separately. Learn how the unified approach delivers a single source of truth, enabling real-time syncing, removing version conflicts, and helping ensure consistent assumptions.

This session is hosted by ASUG.

    • Wednesday, May 13, 2:00 PM - 2:45 PM EDT

entry.title: Transforming SAP APM Documentation for a Global Audience
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) documentation is enhanced to make it more accessible and multilingual. Building on earlier enhancements like SAP Companion and video tutorials, the MT On-the-Fly for Delta Content (MT OTF Delta) marks a leap forward — ensuring accuracy, consistency, and up to date content across all delivery channels.

What This Means for You

  • Always current: Documentation updates happen at the same pace as product changes. Documentation pages available in 39+ languages.
  • Multilingual access: Read content in your preferred language without waiting for translations.
  • Ease of use: Switch between languages anytime, with English always available as the official reference.

Key Enhancements

  • March 2603 release
    • SAP Companion In‑App Help: Context‑sensitive panels, orientation snippets, and direct links to the SAP Help Portal.
    • Video Tutorials: Step‑by‑step guidance to help users learn processes while working.
  • Machine Translation Evolution
  • February 2025: Machine Translation on the Fly (MT on the Fly) introduced dynamic multilingual documentation, allowing users to access content in their preferred language.
  • April 2604: Building on this foundation, MT On-the-Fly for Delta Content (MT OTF Delta) delivered a leap forward. MTOTF enhances accuracy and consistency across all delivery channels, expanding usability worldwide. With this, users will gain faster access to reliable documentation in their preferred language.

Disclaimer: Keep in mind that this is an AI automatic translation tool based on the English Language. Be cautious with the translated content as it may not be perfect. When in doubt, consult the original English text.  

 How to Access Multilingual Documentation

  1. Open the SAP Help Portal → SAP Asset Performance Management | SAP Help Portal
  2. Select Your Language
    • Use the language dropdown at the top of the page (next to “Version: Cloud | English”).
    • Click the arrow and choose from available languages drop-down menu.
    • Select the desired language such as Arabic, Chinese, French, German, Spanish, and more.
  3. Switch Back to English Anytime

Conclusion

The Machine Translation On-the-Fly allows instant translation in 40 languages for SAP APM Help Portal content, enhancing your experience as you can now choose your preferred language. 

With MTOTF, SAP APM documentation is becoming more connected, consistent, and globally accessible.

📌See Also

  • SAP Help Portal Content with Machine Translation On-the-Fly
  • Read the Right Docs: sIoT vs. eIoT in SAP Asset Performance Management
